# Transcrição: 1. CAPI- Events Manager & CAPI Analysis

- **Duração:** 08:28

## [00:00 - 03:02]

So what I want to show you in this video is how to ensure that you have your events successfully set up. So what you want to do is click into this menu item on the left, click into Events Manager and make sure that your settings here are correct. So when you click into Events Manager for the account that you're currently in, you'll see the pixels associated with that ad account on the left hand side. What you want to do is click on the pixel that you're currently working with on the account and you should get a screen similar to this. So at the top you have a few options here. We'll go into these shortly, the activity across your pixel and some information on your integration. So the first thing you want to do is make sure that you have the conversions API active. If you don't you need to get that installed. If you have the conversion API active, you want to make sure it is working properly. So there's a few things that you can do here is just go through the integration paths, make sure everything here is set up properly. I'm not going to go through everything in detail here because you can follow the Facebook guide. If it's beyond your understanding, get someone technical involved, but make sure this is set up properly. If you're integrating through something like Shopify, then the integration is fairly automatic, so you don't need to worry about this. If you're setting up through another landing page builder or a website tool, make sure that it is reporting properly. In particular, the events should be set up in such a way that they are duplicated. You don't want events coming through the conversion API and the pixel itself and being double counted. So that's something you need to keep an eye on. So if you scroll further down, you'll be able to see which events are being recorded and which events are being recorded by the browser, i.e. the pixel and the server. So if you have a cappy conversion API in store, you should have both of these shown for the events that are being sent. Some events may not be sent by your platform. So this ad account is attached to Shopify. Shopify is not sending these and that's fine. I don't need those. The other ones are browser and server. Now the other part that you may look at and be slightly concerned about, I know I see this often, is event match quality. All this is telling you is how much data is being passed via the pixel and or browser back to Facebook. For it to get 10 out of 10, your website would need to send everything that Facebook ever wanted when it comes to data, which rarely happens. And you'll notice here this score of 7.8 on the purchase page is fairly standard.

## [03:02 - 06:05]

This may change over time, you know, depending on when you watch this video, maybe data requirements change from Facebook or whatever. But right now, 7 to 8 is about the right range for your purchase pixel because in Ecom, the more the customer progresses down the funnel, i.e. they go from landing on your page to buying, the more data they're giving up. So it's normal that the purchase page has more data. It's normal that the view content pixel hasn't got as much data, etc. And if you click into this, it will also tell you what data it's collecting and how much of that is being passed through. So if you look at this one here, there are five recommendations. It says if we add data birth, we could get the 7.8 higher. If we add the click ID, this is really important. The FBC ID has additional data every time someone clicks an ad, which you can pull through using something like, I believe, Google Tag Manager. It's a developer level thing. The Facebook login. So if you have, for example, a login that someone logs into your site via Facebook, that can help not many people use that in Ecom. The browser ID is also useful here and the gender. Now, when you look at this, what Facebook are trying to do is identify that user in their database. So we don't, in particular, on this example, have these five data sets. You can see it's currently connected by Shopify. But what are we sending across? We're sending across the email, the IP address, the phone number. There is a slight warning here to say that the phone number is not being sent in the way that Facebook needs it. So that's a slight problem. There's other items being passed. This will capture really 78% of the users. It's kind of what Facebook are trying to say. If you add and you enhance the data, I think, you know, really the click ID, browser ID are probably the most important from these two data birth can help. But really, the email address is a big identifier. After that is the phone number because the email address that people log into Facebook is sometimes different to how they purchase online. Phone numbers often the same unless it's been changed. So a 7.8 is a good score. It's nothing to be stressed out by if you have a developer, then you can squeeze a few things in through that as well. But really, if you're integrating through Shopify, more than likely this is enough. So you don't need to overly stress on that. But the other thing to look at is deduplication. This is so important. What you want to know here is that my events coming through are not being double counted. So what they, what you're looking for is what the rate of deduplication is. And what's actually happening in terms of deduplication. So here, for example, it's saying that 89% of the server data sent was duplicated.

## [06:06 - 08:28]

And it says here it's ideal to have a high rate of do deduplication for server events. It's and again, what Facebook do right now is they prioritize the browser. So if there's two events sent through, so two purchase events via your pixel on your server, Facebook prioritize the pixel event has been the preference. And if it's not available, then it's here. Here it says 11% was not duplicated, which means that 11% of server data coming through wasn't captured in the browser. Here then you're looking at any issues. It will tell you here, Facebook will tell you if there's any issues in double counting. So if you do experience a problem where you're worried about the data and maybe there's something wrong, then it will come over here. So you can see that there's a 94% overlap, which suggests that there's data coming through the API that's not in the pixel, which for most people would be the case that these IDs are being sent by the pixel. This is being sent by the API. So there's no real problem here. So this is a good thing. Then here is how often that data is being sent on higher volume sites. Often it slows down to an hourly on lower volume sites, real time. Daily, weekly, it can be configured, Shopify by default handles this. And you can see any kind of recent activity on the events that are coming through here and you can see what's coming through in terms of parameters and things like that. But that's kind of not that important at this stage. So what we really want to make sure here is that there are no issues. Here you can see, for example, 11,000 events were sent through. Six and a half via the browser, four and a half via the conversion API. All is good for this. So really the key thing here is to make sure, A, that the conversion API is installed. And B, to make sure there are no issues in your reporting. Just to make sure that you're in a good position to get started on your analysis. So without the conversion API, you're going to miss a lot of data. Some point in the future, maybe the pixel completely disappears. But in any case, start that data collection now because you cannot right now backfill the conversion data. So get your CAPI set up and then you'll be in a stronger position to analyze moving forward.
